Notably, the reference-free reward achieves performance comparable to the reference-guided approach, demonstrating that stable and spectrally consistent filtering strategies can be learned even without DNS data, drastically reducing the computational costs of the training phase. The proposed framework provides a robust and flexible alternative to manually tuned regularization parameters, enabling adaptive, physically consistent control of filtering in turbulent flow simulations.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a **Reinforcement Learning (RL)** framework for the dynamic selection of filter parameters in **Evolve-Filter (EF)** regularization strategies for incompressible turbulent flows.
- Implements an **RL agent** that adaptively controls filtering intensity to maintain a balance between numerical stability and physical accuracy.
- Introduces both **reference-guided** (DNS-dependent) and **reference-free** (physics-based) reward formulations to train the model across diverse benchmark problems.
- Demonstrates that the framework prevents **numerical blow-up** while avoiding the excessive dissipation typical of standard fixed-scale filtering methods.
key_results: RL agent successfully learns adaptive filtering policies for flow past a cylinder and decaying turbulence.
Reference-free reward formulations achieve performance parity with DNS-guided training.
Proposed method significantly reduces computational costs by eliminating the need for high-fidelity reference data.
Learned policies maintain spectral consistency and capture large-scale structures accurately.
The framework exhibits robust extrapolation capabilities beyond the specific time windows used during training.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers develop an RL framework that adaptively controls turbulence filters using physics-based rewards without requiring reference data.

title: Modeling Grammatical Hypothesis Testing in Young Learners: A Sequence-Based Learning Analytics Study of Morphosyntactic Reasoning in an Interactive Game
summary: This study investigates grammatical reasoning in primary school learners through a sequence-based learning analytics approach, leveraging fine-grained action sequences from an interactive game targeting morphosyntactic agreement in French. Unlike traditional assessments that rely on final answers, we treat each slider movement as a hypothesis-testing action, capturing real-time cognitive strategies during sentence construction. Analyzing 597 gameplay sessions (9,783 actions) from 100 students aged 8-11 in authentic classroom settings, we introduce Hamming distance to quantify proximity to valid grammatical solutions and examine convergence patterns across exercises with varying levels of difficulty. Results reveal that determiners and verbs are key sites of difficulty, with action sequences deviating from left-to-right usual treatment. This suggests learners often fix the verb first and adjust preceding elements. Exercises with fewer solutions exhibit slower and more erratic convergence, while changes in the closest valid solution indicate dynamic hypothesis revision. Our findings demonstrate how sequence-based analytics can uncover hidden dimensions of linguistic reasoning, offering a foundation for real-time scaffolding and teacher-facing tools in linguistically diverse classrooms.

processed.summary:
• Leverages **sequence-based learning analytics** to model the cognitive strategies of primary learners by analyzing fine-grained action sequences during grammatical tasks.
• Utilizes **Hamming distance** as a primary metric to quantify the proximity of user-generated states to valid grammatical solutions, enabling the tracking of real-time reasoning trajectories.
• Reveals **non-linear processing patterns** in morphosyntactic agreement, where students prioritize anchoring verbs before adjusting preceding sentence elements, deviating from traditional left-to-right models.
• Provides a technical foundation for **real-time scaffolding** and AI-driven diagnostic tools in educational environments by monitoring dynamic hypothesis revision.
key_results: Determiners and verbs are the most significant cognitive bottlenecks in French morphosyntactic agreement tasks.
Student action sequences frequently prioritize fixing the verb first, indicating non-sequential reasoning strategies.
Task difficulty and constraints (fewer solutions) correlate with slower and more erratic convergence toward valid grammar.
Fluctuations in the closest valid solution metric serve as an effective proxy for identifying dynamic hypothesis testing.
Sequence analytics uncover hidden reasoning dimensions that are invisible to traditional end-state assessment methods.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate how sequence-based analytics and Hamming distance quantify non-linear grammatical reasoning and hypothesis testing in young learners.

title: $π$-StepNFT: Wider Space Needs Finer Steps in Online RL for Flow-based VLAs
summary: Flow-based vision-language-action (VLA) models excel in embodied control but suffer from intractable likelihoods during multi-step sampling, hindering online reinforcement learning. We propose \textbf{\textit{$\boldsymbolπ$-StepNFT}} (Step-wise Negative-aware Fine-Tuning), a critic-and-likelihood-free framework that requires only a single forward pass per optimization step and eliminates auxiliary value networks. We identify that wider exploration spaces necessitate finer-grained, step-wise guidance for alignment. Empirically, $π$-StepNFT unlocks latent potential on LIBERO with competitive few-shot robustness. Moreover, it achieves superior generalization on ManiSkill, outperforming value-based baselines in OOD scenarios by preventing overfitting to multimodal features. This property offers a scalable solution promising for complex real-world applications.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **pi-StepNFT**, a critic-and-likelihood-free framework designed to optimize **flow-based Vision-Language-Action (VLA)** models for embodied control.
- Addresses the computational intractability of sampling likelihoods in online RL by utilizing a **single forward pass** per optimization step, removing the need for auxiliary **value networks**.
- Implements **step-wise negative-aware fine-tuning** to provide finer-grained alignment guidance, which is essential for navigation within wide exploration spaces.
- Enhances model **generalization** and **OOD performance** on robotics benchmarks by mitigating overfitting to complex multimodal features.
key_results: Achieves competitive few-shot robustness on the LIBERO benchmark suite.
Outperforms traditional value-based RL baselines in out-of-distribution (OOD) scenarios.
Eliminates the need for complex likelihood estimation, enabling more efficient online optimization.
Demonstrates superior performance on ManiSkill by preventing feature-level overfitting.
Validates that wider exploration spaces require finer, step-wise guidance for successful policy alignment.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce pi-StepNFT to enable efficient, critic-free online reinforcement learning for flow-based VLA models in embodied robotics.

title: MMNavAgent: Multi-Magnification WSI Navigation Agent for Clinically Consistent Whole-Slide Analysis
summary: Recent AI navigation approaches aim to improve Whole-Slide Image (WSI) diagnosis by modeling spatial exploration and selecting diagnostically relevant regions, yet most operate at a single fixed magnification or rely on predefined magnification traversal. In clinical practice, pathologists examine slides across multiple magnifications and selectively inspect only necessary scales, dynamically integrating global and cellular evidence in a sequential manner. This mismatch prevents existing methods from modeling cross-magnification interactions and adaptive magnification selection inherent to real diagnostic workflows. To these, we propose a clinically consistent Multi-Magnification WSI Navigation Agent (MMNavAgent) that explicitly models multi magnification interaction and adaptive magnification selection. Specifically, we introduce a Cross-Magnification navigation Tool (CMT) that aggregates contextual information from adjacent magnifications to enhance discriminative representations along the navigation path. We further introduce a Magnification Selection Tool (MST) that leverages memory-driven reasoning within the agent framework to enable interactive and adaptive magnification selection, mimicking the sequential decision process of pathologists. Extensive experiments on a public dataset demonstrate improved diagnostic performance, with 1.45% gain of AUC and 2.93% gain of BACC over a non-agent baseline. Code will be public upon acceptance.

processed.summary:
• Introduces **MMNavAgent**, a framework mimicking pathologist workflows via **adaptive magnification selection** and multi-scale spatial exploration.
• Features a **Cross-Magnification Tool (CMT)** that aggregates context from adjacent scales to enrich discriminative representations during navigation.
• Implements a **Magnification Selection Tool (MST)** leveraging **memory-driven reasoning** to dynamically decide optimal resolution for specific slide regions.
• Improves clinical consistency by transitioning from fixed-magnification models to **sequential decision-making** agentic architectures.
key_results: Achieved a 1.45% gain in AUC over non-agent baselines.
Realized a 2.93% improvement in Balanced Accuracy (BACC).
Successfully modeled cross-magnification interactions within a single diagnostic path.
Demonstrated the effectiveness of memory-driven reasoning for adaptive scale selection.
Validated clinical consistency by mimicking the sequential decision process of human pathologists.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce MMNavAgent, employing memory-driven reasoning for adaptive magnification selection to enhance clinical whole-slide image analysis.

title: When an AI Judges Your Work: The Hidden Costs of Algorithmic Assessment
summary: We use an online experiment with a real work task to study whether workers change their behavior when they know AI will be used to judge their work instead of humans. We find that individuals produce a higher quantity of output when they are assigned an AI evaluator. However, controlling for quantity, the quality of their output is lower, regardless of whether quality is measured using humans or LLM grades. We also find that workers are more likely to use external tools, including LLMs, when they know AI is used to judge their work instead of humans. However, the increase in external tool use does not appear to explain the differences in quantity or quality across treatments.

processed.summary:
• Investigates the impact of **Algorithmic Assessment** on worker behavior, comparing human-led evaluation against automated **AI judges**.
• Identifies a distinct shift in labor patterns where workers prioritize **output volume** at the expense of substantive **output quality**.
• Explores the "gaming" of **AI Evaluation** systems through increased reliance on **external LLM tools** during task execution.
• Provides critical evidence for the **Generative AI Trends** space regarding the limitations of using LLMs as objective proxies for complex work performance.
key_results: Workers assigned an AI evaluator produce a significantly higher quantity of output.
Output quality is lower under AI evaluation, regardless of whether a human or LLM grades it.
Individuals are more likely to use external tools like ChatGPT when they know an AI is judging them.
The increase in external tool usage does not explain the observed drop in quality or increase in quantity.
Workers adjust their effort strategies to optimize for perceived algorithmic shortcuts.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that AI-led evaluation incentivizes workers to increase output volume while significantly decreasing overall work quality.

title: Trident: Adaptive Scheduling for Heterogeneous Multimodal Data Pipelines
summary: The rapid adoption of large language models and multimodal foundation models has made multimodal data preparation pipelines critical AI infrastructure. These pipelines interleave CPU-heavy preprocessing with accelerator-backed (GPU/NPU/TPU) inference and produce massive intermediate artifacts. Achieving high throughput is difficult because workloads are highly non-stationary: regime shifts, input-dependent inference, and transient memory spikes cause rapid performance fluctuations and out-of-memory (OOM) failures. Existing schedulers typically rely on threshold-based autoscaling or assume synchronous, homogeneous operators, leading to poor efficiency. We present Trident, an adaptive scheduling framework for heterogeneous multimodal pipelines on fixed-resource clusters. Trident closes the loop across three coupled layers: (i) an observation layer that estimates per-operator sustainable throughput for asynchronous operators via Gaussian Process regression with anomaly filtering; (ii) an adaptation layer that detects workload shifts online and performs memory-constrained Bayesian optimization to recommend OOM-safe configurations; and (iii) a scheduling layer that solves a mixed-integer linear program to jointly optimize operator parallelism, placement, and configuration transitions under heterogeneous compute and bandwidth constraints, accounting for cold-start overhead via rolling updates. Decisions trigger sample invalidation and model refresh to keep estimates consistent with the active configuration. Implemented on Ray Data, Trident improves end-to-end throughput by up to 2.01x on a document curation (PDF) pipeline and 1.88x on a video curation pipeline over a static baseline, with low overhead suitable for online re-optimization.

processed.summary:
• Trident introduces an **adaptive scheduling framework** designed for heterogeneous multimodal pipelines, bridging the gap between CPU-bound preprocessing and accelerator-backed inference.
• The architecture features an observation layer using **Gaussian Process regression** for throughput estimation and an adaptation layer utilizing **Bayesian optimization** for OOM-safe configurations.
• It employs a **Mixed-Integer Linear Program (MILP)** at the scheduling layer to jointly optimize operator parallelism, placement, and transitions under compute and bandwidth constraints.
• Built on **Ray Data**, the system addresses non-stationary workloads and transient memory spikes through online re-optimization and rolling updates.
key_results: Improved end-to-end throughput by 2.01x on document curation (PDF) pipelines.
Achieved 1.88x throughput improvement on video curation pipelines over static baselines.
Successfully mitigated out-of-memory (OOM) failures using memory-constrained optimization.
Maintained low overhead suitable for real-time online re-optimization of active configurations.
Effective handling of regime shifts and input-dependent inference fluctuations in multimodal workloads.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce Trident, an adaptive framework that doubles multimodal pipeline throughput via MILP-based scheduling and Bayesian optimization.

title: Explainability and justification of automatic-decision making: A conceptual framework and a practical application
summary: Explainability of algorithmic decision-making systems is both a regulatory objective and an area of intense research. The article argues that a crucial condition for the acceptability of algorithmic decision-making systems is that decisions must be justified in the eyes of their recipients. We make a clear distinction between explanation and justification. Explanations describe how a decision was made, while justifications give reasons that aim to make the decision acceptable. We propose a conceptual framework of explanations and justifications, based on Habermas's theory of communicative action and Perelman's New Rhetoric theory of law. This framework helps to analyze how different forms of explanation can support or fail to support justification. We illustrate our approach with a case study on university admissions in France.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a **sociotechnical framework** that distinguishes between technical **explanations** (causal descriptions of how models work) and **justifications** (reasons that make a decision normative and acceptable).
- Integrates **Habermas's theory of communicative action** and **Perelman's New Rhetoric** to evaluate the legitimacy of automated decision-making (ADM) systems beyond mere transparency.
- Bridges the gap between **algorithmic interpretability** and legal requirements by analyzing how feature-based explanations fail or succeed in satisfying the expectations of decision recipients.
key_results: Formal distinction between mechanistic explanation and normative justification in AI.
Mapping of technical local/global explanations to social validity claims.
Identification of the 'justification gap' in current black-box ADM implementations.
A conceptual bridge between AI transparency and the legal theory of justification.
Case study application demonstrating framework utility in the French university admission context.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers propose a framework distinguishing algorithmic explanations from justifications to improve the social acceptability of AI decisions.

title: Cognitive Prosthetic: An AI-Enabled Multimodal System for Episodic Recall in Knowledge Work
summary: Modern knowledge workplaces increasingly strain human episodic memory as individuals navigate fragmented attention, overlapping meetings, and multimodal information streams. Existing workplace tools provide partial support through note-taking or analytics but rarely integrate cognitive, physiological, and attentional context into retrievable memory representations. This paper presents the Cognitive Prosthetic Multimodal System (CPMS) --an AI-enabled proof-of-concept designed to support episodic recall in knowledge work through structured episodic capture and natural language retrieval. CPMS synchronizes speech transcripts, physiological signals, and gaze behavior into temporally aligned, JSON-based episodic records processed locally for privacy. Beyond data logging, the system includes a web-based retrieval interface that allows users to query past workplace experiences using natural language, referencing semantic content, time, attentional focus, or physiological state. We present CPMS as a functional proof-of-concept demonstrating the technical feasibility of transforming heterogeneous sensor data into queryable episodic memories. The system is designed to be modular, supporting operation with partial sensor configurations, and incorporates privacy safeguards for workplace deployment. This work contributes an end-to-end, privacy-aware architecture for AI-enabled memory augmentation in workplace settings.

processed.summary:
* The **Cognitive Prosthetic Multimodal System (CPMS)** integrates speech transcripts, **physiological signals**, and gaze behavior into a unified, temporally aligned **JSON-based episodic record**.
* The architecture prioritizes **local processing** to ensure data privacy, transforming heterogeneous sensor data into a structured format for **natural language retrieval**.
* A web-based interface allows users to perform **semantic queries** on past workplace experiences, referencing not just content but also **attentional focus** and physiological states.
key_results: Development of an end-to-end architecture for multimodal episodic memory augmentation.
Temporal alignment of disparate data streams including gaze, audio, and bio-signals.
Implementation of a privacy-first, local-only processing pipeline for sensitive workplace data.
Creation of a modular system design that maintains functionality with partial sensor configurations.
Proof-of-concept demonstration of natural language querying for cross-modal memory recall.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce CPMS, a multimodal system using local AI to transform physiological and sensory data into queryable workplace memories.

title: Exploring Plan Space through Conversation: An Agentic Framework for LLM-Mediated Explanations in Planning
summary: When automating plan generation for a real-world sequential decision problem, the goal is often not to replace the human planner, but to facilitate an iterative reasoning and elicitation process, where the human's role is to guide the AI planner according to their preferences and expertise. In this context, explanations that respond to users' questions are crucial to improve their understanding of potential solutions and increase their trust in the system. To enable natural interaction with such a system, we present a multi-agent Large Language Model (LLM) architecture that is agnostic to the explanation framework and enables user- and context-dependent interactive explanations. We also describe an instantiation of this framework for goal-conflict explanations, which we use to conduct a user study comparing the LLM-powered interaction with a baseline template-based explanation interface.

processed.summary:
• Proposes a **multi-agent LLM architecture** designed to bridge the gap between symbolic AI planners and human users through conversational interfaces.
• The framework is **explanation-agnostic**, allowing it to integrate with various formal planning solvers while providing context-aware, user-dependent reasoning.
• Utilizes an **agentic workflow** to translate technical goal conflicts and plan constraints into natural language, facilitating iterative plan elicitation.
• Focuses on **human-AI collaboration**, ensuring the human remains in the loop to guide the planning process based on personal expertise and preferences.
key_results: Developed a modular multi-agent system that decouples formal planning logic from natural language generation.
Successfully implemented an instantiation for goal-conflict explanations in complex plan spaces.
Enabled interactive 'elicitation' where users can query and refine plans through dialogue.
User study results showed a preference for the LLM-powered interaction over traditional template-based systems.
Demonstrated that LLMs can effectively serve as mediators for formal reasoning systems without losing technical accuracy.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce a multi-agent LLM framework that translates complex automated planning data into interactive, conversational user explanations.

title: OpenRad: a Curated Repository of Open-access AI models for Radiology
summary: The rapid developments in artificial intelligence (AI) research in radiology have produced numerous models that are scattered across various platforms and sources, limiting discoverability, reproducibility and clinical translation. Herein, OpenRad was created, a curated, standardized, open-access repository that aggregates radiology AI models and providing details such as the availability of pretrained weights and interactive applications. Retrospective analysis of peer reviewed literature and preprints indexed in PubMed, arXiv and Scopus was performed until Dec 2025 (n = 5239 records). Model records were generated using a locally hosted LLM (gpt-oss:120b), based on the RSNA AI Roadmap JSON schema, and manually verified by ten expert reviewers. Stability of LLM outputs was assessed on 225 randomly selected papers using text similarity metrics. A total of 1694 articles were included after review. Included models span all imaging modalities (CT, MRI, X-ray, US) and radiology subspecialties. Automated extraction demonstrated high stability for structured fields (Levenshtein ratio > 90%), with 78.5% of record edits being characterized as minor during expert review. Statistical analysis of the repository revealed CNN and transformer architectures as dominant, while MRI was the most commonly used modality (in 621 neuroradiology AI models). Research output was mostly concentrated in China and the United States. The OpenRad web interface enables model discovery via keyword search and filters for modality, subspecialty, intended use, verification status and demo availability, alongside live statistics. The community can contribute new models through a dedicated portal. OpenRad contains approx. 1700 open access, curated radiology AI models with standardized metadata, supplemented with analysis of code repositories, thereby creating a comprehensive, searchable resource for the radiology community.

processed.summary:
* OpenRad establishes a **standardized, curated repository** of approximately 1,700 open-access radiology AI models to address fragmentation and improve research reproducibility.
* The methodology utilized a **locally hosted LLM (gpt-oss:120b)** to automate data extraction into the **RSNA AI Roadmap JSON schema**, followed by expert human verification.
* Architectural trends within the repository show that **CNN and Transformer architectures** remain dominant, particularly within the 621 neuroradiology models identified.
* The system facilitates **Multimodal AI discovery** through a web interface that filters by imaging modality, subspecialty, and availability of pretrained weights or interactive demos.
key_results: Processed 5,239 records to curate 1,694 high-quality radiology AI articles and models.
Automated LLM extraction achieved high stability with a Levenshtein ratio exceeding 90% for structured fields.
Expert reviewers characterized 78.5% of LLM-generated record edits as minor, validating the automated curation workflow.
MRI was identified as the most common imaging modality, featured in over 600 specific models.
Research output was found to be heavily concentrated in China and the United States.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: OpenRad researchers introduce a curated, LLM-indexed repository to centralize 1,700 open-access radiology models and standardize medical AI metadata.

title: Beyond Microservices: Testing Web-Scale RCA Methods on GPU-Driven LLM Workloads
summary: Large language model (LLM) services have become an integral part of search, assistance, and decision-making applications. However, unlike traditional web or microservices, the hardware and software stack enabling LLM inference deployment is of higher complexity and far less field-tested, making it more susceptible to failures that are difficult to resolve. Keeping outage costs and quality of service degradations in check depends on shortening mean time to repair, which in practice is gated by how quickly the fault is identified, located, and diagnosed. Automated root cause analysis (RCA) accelerates failure localization by identifying the system component that failed and tracing how the failure propagated. Numerous RCA methods have been developed for traditional services, using request path tracing, resource metric and log data analysis. Yet, existing RCA methods have not been designed for LLM deployments that present distinct runtime characteristics. In this study, we evaluate the effectiveness of RCA methods on a best-practice LLM inference deployment under controlled failure injections. Across 24 methods (20 metric-based, two trace-based, and two multi-source), we find that multi-source approaches achieve the highest accuracy, metric-based methods show fault-type-dependent performance, and trace-based methods largely fail. These results reveal that existing RCA tools do not generalize to LLM systems, motivating tailored analysis techniques and enhanced observability, for which we formulate guidelines.

processed.summary:
- Evaluates **Root Cause Analysis (RCA)** frameworks on GPU-intensive LLM inference deployments, highlighting the architectural shift from traditional microservices to hardware-bound AI stacks.
- Benchmarked **24 distinct methods** (20 metric-based, 2 trace-based, and 2 multi-source) under controlled failure injections to assess diagnostic accuracy.
- Identifies that **trace-based methods** largely fail due to the unique asynchronous execution and complex dependencies inherent in LLM inference.
- Establishes a foundation for **tailored observability guidelines** designed to reduce Mean Time to Repair (MTTR) for web-scale generative AI applications.
key_results: Multi-source RCA approaches achieved the highest diagnostic accuracy across all tested failure modes.
Metric-based methods exhibited inconsistent performance that fluctuated based on the specific type of fault injected.
Trace-based methods proved ineffective for LLM workloads, failing to generalize beyond traditional web services.
The high complexity of the GPU hardware/software stack makes standard microservice RCA tools obsolete for LLM deployments.
Proposed guidelines emphasize the need for LLM-specific telemetry that accounts for distinct runtime characteristics.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that traditional RCA methods fail on LLM workloads, necessitating multi-source observability for reliable fault diagnosis.

title: "When to Hand Off, When to Work Together": Expanding Human-Agent Co-Creative Collaboration through Concurrent Interaction
summary: Human collaborators coordinate dynamically through process visibility and workspace awareness, yet AI agents typically either provide only final outputs or expose read-only execution processes (e.g., planning, reasoning) without interpreting concurrent user actions on shared artifacts. Building on mixed-initiative interaction principles, we explore whether agents can achieve collaborative context awareness -- interpreting concurrent user actions on shared artifacts and adapting in real-time. Study 1 (N=10 professional designers) revealed that process visibility enabled reasoning about agent actions but exposed conflicts when agents could not distinguish feedback from independent work. We developed CLEO, which interprets collaborative intent and adapts in real-time. Study 2 (N=10, two-day with stimulated recall interviews) analyzed 214 turns, identifying five action patterns, six triggers, and four enabling factors explaining when designers choose delegation (70.1%), direction (28.5%), or concurrent work (31.8%). We present a decision model with six interaction loops, design implications, and an annotated dataset.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a shift from read-only AI execution to **Mixed-Initiative Interaction**, where agents interpret and react to concurrent user actions on shared artifacts.
- Introduces **CLEO**, a framework designed to achieve **collaborative context awareness** by reasoning about user intent and adapting its process in real-time.
- Formalizes a **decision model** featuring six interaction loops that categorize the fluid transitions between human delegation, direction, and synchronized work.
key_results: Identified five distinct action patterns and six triggers defining human-agent co-creation.
Found that designers engage in concurrent work 31.8% of the time when agents provide process visibility.
Developed the CLEO system to resolve conflicts by distinguishing feedback from independent user work.
Categorized 214 turns of interaction into a model of delegation (70.1%) versus direction (28.5%).
Provided an annotated dataset and design implications for the next generation of co-creative AI tools.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce CLEO to enable agents to interpret concurrent user actions and adapt during real-time co-creative tasks.

title: NICO-RAG: Multimodal Hypergraph Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Understanding the Nicotine Public Health Crisis
summary: The nicotine addiction public health crisis continues to be pervasive. In this century alone, the tobacco industry has released and marketed new products in an aggressive effort to lure new and young customers for life. Such innovations and product development, namely flavored nicotine or tobacco such as nicotine pouches, have undone years of anti-tobacco campaign work. Past work is limited both in scope and in its ability to connect large-scale data points. Thus, we introduce the Nicotine Innovation Counter-Offensive (NICO) Dataset to provide public health researchers with over 200,000 multimodal samples, including images and text descriptions, on 55 tobacco and nicotine product brands. In addition, to provide public health researchers with factual connections across a large-scale dataset, we propose NICO-RAG, a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) framework that can retrieve image features without incurring the high-cost of language models, as well as the added cost of processing image tokens with large-scale datasets such as NICO. At construction time, NICO-RAG organizes image- and text-extracted entities and relations into hypergraphs to produce as factual responses as possible. This joint multimodal knowledge representation enables NICO-RAG to retrieve images for query answering not only by visual similarity but also by the semantic similarity of image descriptions. Experimentals show that without needing to process additional tokens from images for over 100 questions, NICO-RAG performs comparably to the state-of-the-art RAG method adapted for images.

processed.summary:
* Introduces the **NICO Dataset**, a large-scale repository featuring over 200,000 multimodal samples across 55 nicotine brands to assist public health research.
* Proposes **NICO-RAG**, a framework that organizes image- and text-extracted entities into **hypergraphs** to ensure factual grounding and complex relationship mapping.
* Implements a **joint multimodal knowledge representation** that allows for retrieval based on both visual similarity and semantic description similarity without the overhead of image token processing.
* Optimizes for **computational efficiency** by bypassing the high cost of processing image tokens in Large Language Models during the retrieval-augmentation phase.
key_results: Creation of a 200,000+ sample multimodal dataset for tracking nicotine product innovation.
Development of a hypergraph-based retrieval system for improved factuality in public health queries.
Achievement of performance comparable to state-of-the-art multimodal RAG methods.
Significant reduction in inference costs by eliminating the need for image token processing.
Successful retrieval of information using both visual features and text-based semantic descriptors.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce NICO-RAG, utilizing hypergraphs for efficient multimodal retrieval without the high cost of processing image tokens.

title: EstLLM: Enhancing Estonian Capabilities in Multilingual LLMs via Continued Pretraining and Post-Training
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are predominantly trained on English-centric data, resulting in uneven performance for smaller languages. We study whether continued pretraining (CPT) can substantially improve Estonian capabilities in a pretrained multilingual LLM while preserving its English and general reasoning performance. Using Llama 3.1 8B as the main base model, we perform CPT on a mixture that increases Estonian exposure while approximating the original training distribution through English replay and the inclusion of code, mathematics, and instruction-like data. We subsequently apply supervised fine-tuning, preference optimization, and chat vector merging to introduce robust instruction-following behavior. Evaluation on a comprehensive suite of Estonian benchmarks shows consistent gains in linguistic competence, knowledge, reasoning, translation quality, and instruction-following compared to the original base model and its instruction-tuned variant, while maintaining competitive performance on English benchmarks. These findings indicate that CPT, with an appropriately balanced data mixture, together with post-training alignment, can substantially improve single-language capabilities in pretrained multilingual LLMs.

processed.summary:
* Utilizes **Llama 3.1 8B** as a base model to perform **Continued Pretraining (CPT)** specifically focused on enhancing Estonian language capabilities.
* Implements a **balanced data mixture** strategy that combines Estonian corpus with English replay, code, and mathematics to prevent **catastrophic forgetting** of general reasoning skills.
* Employs a comprehensive post-training pipeline including **Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)**, **Preference Optimization**, and **chat vector merging** to ensure robust instruction-following behavior.
key_results: Consistent improvement in Estonian linguistic competence and domain-specific knowledge.
Significant gains in Estonian translation quality over the base Llama 3.1 8B model.
Successful preservation of English reasoning and general performance through English replay data.
Enhanced instruction-following capabilities in Estonian through strategic preference optimization.
Validated superiority on a comprehensive suite of Estonian-specific evaluation benchmarks.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: University of Tartu researchers enhance Llama 3.1's Estonian capabilities using continued pretraining and chat vector merging without degrading English performance.

title: LAD-Drive: Bridging Language and Trajectory with Action-Aware Diffusion Transformers
summary: While multimodal large language models (MLLMs) provide advanced reasoning for autonomous driving, translating their discrete semantic knowledge into continuous trajectories remains a fundamental challenge. Existing methods often rely on unimodal planning heads that inherently limit their ability to represent multimodal driving behavior. Furthermore, most generative approaches frequently condition on one-hot encoded actions, discarding the nuanced navigational uncertainty critical for complex scenarios. To resolve these limitations, we introduce LAD-Drive, a generative framework that structurally disentangles high-level intention from low-level spatial planning. LAD-Drive employs an action decoder to infer a probabilistic meta-action distribution, establishing an explicit belief state that preserves the nuanced intent typically lost by one-hot encodings. This distribution, fused with the vehicle's kinematic state, conditions an action-aware diffusion decoder that utilizes a truncated denoising process to refine learned motion anchors into safe, kinematically feasible trajectories. Extensive evaluations on the LangAuto benchmark demonstrate that LAD-Drive achieves state-of-the-art results, outperforming competitive baselines by up to 59% in Driving Score while significantly reducing route deviations and collisions. We will publicly release the code and models on https://github.com/iis-esslingen/lad-drive.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **LAD-Drive**, a generative framework that bridges the gap between discrete MLLM semantic reasoning and continuous trajectory planning via **Action-Aware Diffusion Transformers**.
- Implements a structural disentanglement of high-level intention from low-level spatial planning, utilizing a probabilistic **action decoder** to establish an explicit belief state.
- Employs a **truncated denoising process** within a diffusion decoder to refine learned motion anchors into kinematically feasible and safe driving trajectories.
- Fuses meta-action distributions with vehicle kinematic states to preserve navigational uncertainty, overcoming the limitations of traditional one-hot action encodings.
key_results: Achieved state-of-the-art performance on the LangAuto autonomous driving benchmark.
Outperformed competitive baselines by up to 59% in Driving Score metrics.
Significantly reduced route deviations through better intent preservation.
Substantially decreased collision rates compared to unimodal planning heads.
Demonstrated successful mapping of MLLM reasoning to complex, continuous motion control.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: IIS Esslingen researchers introduce LAD-Drive, using action-aware diffusion to transform MLLM reasoning into superior autonomous driving trajectories.

title: Decoherence and entropy production due to quantum fluctuations of spacetime
summary: The intersection between quantum mechanics and gravitational physics has been providing challenging puzzles for decades. In this thesis, we study the dynamics of an open quantum system coupled with a bath of gravitons, the quanta of the gravitational field in the linear limit of general relativity. We focus on two main aspects. First, we analyze the decoherence induced by gravitons when we consider the open system to be described by both external and internal degrees of freedom. Since gravity is universal, the internal variables also interact with the gravitons, and here we show that this interaction leads to the decoherence of spatial superpositions of microscopic systems in the long-time regime, even when the graviton bath alone does not. We then proceed to the second main aspect, which is the entropy production that arises when an external agent drives a quantum system through the graviton bath. This irreversibility comes from quantum fluctuations of spacetime itself and, as such, has a fundamentally universal aspect.

processed.summary:
• Investigates the dynamics of an **open quantum system** coupled to a bath of **gravitons** within the linear limit of general relativity.
• Analyzes how **internal degrees of freedom** contribute to the **decoherence of spatial superpositions** in microscopic systems over long-time regimes.
• Examines **entropy production** and irreversibility driven by external agents interacting with universal **quantum fluctuations of spacetime**.
key_results: Gravity's universality ensures internal variables contribute to system decoherence.
Spatial superpositions of microscopic systems decohere in the long-time regime via graviton interaction.
External agents driving quantum systems through graviton baths result in measurable entropy production.
Irreversibility is identified as a fundamental consequence of spacetime fluctuations.
The study provides a theoretical framework for the intersection of linear general relativity and open quantum systems.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate how quantum fluctuations in spacetime induce universal decoherence and entropy production in open quantum systems.

title: MetaRCA: A Generalizable Root Cause Analysis Framework for Cloud-Native Systems Powered by Meta Causal Knowledge
summary: The dynamics and complexity of cloud-native systems present significant challenges for Root Cause Analysis (RCA). While causality-based RCA methods have shown significant progress in recent years, their practical adoption is fundamentally limited by three intertwined challenges: poor scalability against system complexity, brittle generalization across different system topologies, and inadequate integration of domain knowledge. These limitations create a vicious cycle, hindering the development of robust and efficient RCA solutions. This paper introduces MetaRCA, a generalizable RCA framework for cloud-native systems. MetaRCA first constructs a Meta Causal Graph (MCG) offline, a reusable knowledge base defined at the metadata level. To build the MCG, we propose an evidence-driven algorithm that systematically fuses knowledge from Large Language Models (LLMs), historical fault reports, and observability data. When a fault occurs, MetaRCA performs a lightweight online inference by dynamically instantiating the MCG into a localized graph based on the current context, and then leverages real-time data to weight and prune causal links for precise root cause localization. Evaluated on 252 public and 59 production failures, MetaRCA demonstrates state-of-the-art performance. It surpasses the strongest baseline by 29 percentage points in service-level and 48 percentage points in metric-level accuracy. This performance advantage widens as system complexity increases, with its overhead scaling near-linearly. Crucially, MetaRCA shows robust cross-system generalization, maintaining over 80% accuracy across diverse systems.

processed.summary:
• Introduces **MetaRCA**, a framework that utilizes a **Meta Causal Graph (MCG)** to resolve the scalability and brittleness of traditional causality-based Root Cause Analysis.
• Features a hybrid **knowledge fusion algorithm** that systematically synthesizes domain knowledge from **LLMs**, historical fault reports, and telemetry data to create a reusable causal metadata layer.
• Employs **lightweight online inference** by dynamically instantiating the MCG into localized graphs, which are then pruned and weighted using real-time observability metrics.
• Provides a **generalizable architecture** that maintains high accuracy across diverse system topologies while maintaining near-linear computational overhead.
key_results: Improved service-level accuracy by 29 percentage points over state-of-the-art baselines.
Achieved a 48 percentage point increase in metric-level root cause localization precision.
Demonstrated robust cross-system generalization with over 80% accuracy on unseen topologies.
Validated performance on a massive dataset of 252 public and 59 production-scale failures.
Maintained near-linear scalability, effectively handling the high complexity of cloud-native environments.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Meta researchers introduce MetaRCA, a causal framework fusing LLM knowledge with observability data to achieve scalable, generalizable root cause analysis.

title: Revealing Combinatorial Reasoning of GNNs via Graph Concept Bottleneck Layer
summary: Despite their success in various domains, the growing dependence on GNNs raises a critical concern about the nature of the combinatorial reasoning underlying their predictions, which is often hidden within their black-box architectures. Addressing this challenge requires understanding how GNNs translate topological patterns into logical rules. However, current works only uncover the hard logical rules over graph concepts, which cannot quantify the contribution of each concept to prediction. Moreover, they are post-hoc interpretable methods that generate explanations after model training and may not accurately reflect the true combinatorial reasoning of GNNs, since they approximate it with a surrogate. In this work, we develop a graph concept bottleneck layer that can be integrated into any GNN architectures to guide them to predict the selected discriminative global graph concepts. The predicted concept scores are further projected to class labels by a sparse linear layer. It enforces the combinatorial reasoning of GNNs' predictions to fit the soft logical rule over graph concepts and thus can quantify the contribution of each concept. To further improve the quality of the concept bottleneck, we treat concepts as "graph words" and graphs as "graph sentences", and leverage language models to learn graph concept embeddings. Extensive experiments on multiple datasets show that our method GCBMs achieve state-of-the-art performance both in classification and interpretability.

processed.summary:
• Introduces the **Graph Concept Bottleneck Layer (GCBM)**, an intrinsic interpretability module that maps GNN topological patterns to **global graph concepts**.
• Employs a **sparse linear layer** to transform predicted concept scores into labels, enabling the quantification of individual concept contributions via **soft logical rules**.
• Leverages **Language Models** to generate graph concept embeddings by framing graphs as "sentences" and concepts as "words," enhancing the bottleneck's semantic depth.
• Provides a **plug-and-play architecture** compatible with any GNN, shifting from post-hoc surrogate explanations to transparent, built-in combinatorial reasoning.
key_results: Development of an architecture-agnostic layer for inherent GNN transparency.
Quantification of concept contributions through soft logical reasoning instead of binary rules.
Application of LMs to treat graphs as "sentences" for superior concept embedding learning.
Achieved state-of-the-art performance in both predictive accuracy and interpretability metrics.
Demonstrated successful integration across multiple standard GNN benchmark datasets.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce GCBMs to make GNNs interpretable by using language models to extract and quantify graph concepts.

title: MMR-Life: Piecing Together Real-life Scenes for Multimodal Multi-image Reasoning
summary: Recent progress in the reasoning capabilities of multimodal large language models (MLLMs) has empowered them to address more complex tasks such as scientific analysis and mathematical reasoning. Despite their promise, MLLMs' reasoning abilities across different scenarios in real life remain largely unexplored and lack standardized benchmarks for evaluation. To address this gap, we introduce MMR-Life, a comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate the diverse multimodal multi-image reasoning capabilities of MLLMs across real-life scenarios. MMR-Life consists of 2,646 multiple-choice questions based on 19,108 images primarily sourced from real-world contexts, comprehensively covering seven reasoning types: abductive, analogical, causal, deductive, inductive, spatial, and temporal. Unlike existing reasoning benchmarks, MMR-Life does not rely on domain-specific expertise but instead requires models to integrate information across multiple images and apply diverse reasoning abilities. The evaluation of 37 advanced models highlights the substantial challenge posed by MMR-Life. Even top models like GPT-5 achieve only 58% accuracy and display considerable variance in performance across reasoning types. Moreover, we analyze the reasoning paradigms of existing MLLMs, exploring how factors such as thinking length, reasoning method, and reasoning type affect their performance. In summary, MMR-Life establishes a comprehensive foundation for evaluating, analyzing, and improving the next generation of multimodal reasoning systems.

processed.summary:
* Introduces **MMR-Life**, a comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate **Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs)** on complex, multi-image reasoning within real-world contexts.
* The framework covers seven distinct **reasoning types** including abductive, spatial, and temporal logic, moving beyond domain-specific expertise to test generalized **information integration**.
* Evaluates 37 state-of-the-art models to establish a baseline for how **thinking length** and reasoning paradigms influence performance in high-dimensional visual tasks.
* Aims to improve the next generation of **multimodal reasoning systems** by identifying architectural bottlenecks in cross-image relationship mapping.
key_results: MMR-Life comprises 2,646 multiple-choice questions based on a dataset of 19,108 real-life images.
The benchmark evaluates seven core reasoning abilities: abductive, analogical, causal, deductive, inductive, spatial, and temporal.
Top-performing models, including GPT-5, achieve a peak accuracy of only 58%, highlighting a major gap in current AI capabilities.

processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce MMR-Life, a benchmark revealing that advanced multimodal models currently fail to master complex real-life reasoning.

title: Selection as Power: Constrained Reinforcement for Bounded Decision Authority
summary:
Selection as Power argued that upstream selection authority, rather than internal objective misalignment, constitutes a primary source of risk in high-stakes agentic systems. However, the original framework was static: governance constraints bounded selection power but did not adapt over time. In this work, we extend the framework to dynamic settings by introducing incentivized selection governance, where reinforcement updates are applied to scoring and reducer parameters under externally enforced sovereignty constraints.
  We formalize selection as a constrained reinforcement process in which parameter updates are projected onto governance-defined feasible sets, preventing concentration beyond prescribed bounds. Across multiple regulated financial scenarios, unconstrained reinforcement consistently collapses into deterministic dominance under repeated feedback, especially at higher learning rates. In contrast, incentivized governance enables adaptive improvement while maintaining bounded selection concentration.
  Projection-based constraints transform reinforcement from irreversible lock-in into controlled adaptation, with governance debt quantifying the tension between optimization pressure and authority bounds. These results demonstrate that learning dynamics can coexist with structural diversity when sovereignty constraints are enforced at every update step, offering a principled approach to integrating reinforcement into high-stakes agentic systems without surrendering bounded selection authority.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **incentivized selection governance**, a framework that extends selection power theory into dynamic reinforcement learning settings.
- Implements **projection-based constraints** on scoring and reducer parameters, ensuring that reinforcement updates are restricted to governance-defined feasible sets.
- Architecture prevents **deterministic dominance**, where agentic systems collapse into irreversible lock-in states under high learning rates.
- Establishes a principled method for integrating RL into **high-stakes agentic systems** by enforcing sovereignty constraints at every update step.
key_results: Unconstrained reinforcement consistently leads to power concentration and deterministic dominance in agentic systems.
Projection-based constraints effectively maintain structural diversity even under high optimization pressure.
The framework enables adaptive performance improvement while strictly honoring authority bounds.
Introduced the concept of 'governance debt' to quantify tension between optimization and sovereignty.
Demonstrated stability in regulated financial scenarios where standard reinforcement models failed to maintain distribution.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce a constrained reinforcement framework to prevent agentic systems from monopolizing selection authority during autonomous learning.

title: Protection against Source Inference Attacks in Federated Learning
summary:
Federated Learning (FL) was initially proposed as a privacy-preserving machine learning paradigm. However, FL has been shown to be susceptible to a series of privacy attacks. Recently, there has been concern about the Source Inference Attack (SIA), where an honest-but-curious central server attempts to identify exactly which client owns a given data point which was used in the training phase. Alarmingly, standard gradient obfuscation techniques with Differential Privacy have been shown to be ineffective against SIAs, at least without severely diminishing the accuracy.
  In this work, we propose a defense against SIAs within the widely studied shuffle model of FL, where an honest shuffler acts as an intermediary between the clients and the server. First, we demonstrate that standard naive shuffling alone is insufficient to prevent SIAs. To effectively defend against SIAs, shuffling needs to be applied at a more granular level; we propose a novel combination of parameter-level shuffling with the residue number system (RNS). Our approach provides robust protection against SIAs without affecting the accuracy of the joint model and can be seamlessly integrated into other privacy protection mechanisms.
  We conduct experiments on a series of models and datasets, confirming that standard shuffling approaches fail to prevent SIAs and that, in contrast, our proposed method reduce the attack's accuracy to the level of random guessing.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a defense against **Source Inference Attacks (SIA)** in Federated Learning using a shuffle model architecture with an intermediary shuffler.
- Introduces a novel combination of **parameter-level shuffling** and the **Residue Number System (RNS)** to obscure client-specific gradient contributions.
- Achieves robust privacy protection without degrading the **joint model's accuracy**, overcoming the primary trade-off found in standard Differential Privacy (DP).
- Demonstrates that granular, parameter-level manipulation is required to break the link between specific data points and their source clients.
key_results: Naive shuffling alone is proven insufficient to prevent sophisticated Source Inference Attacks.
The proposed RNS-based shuffling reduces attack accuracy to the level of random guessing.
Unlike Differential Privacy, this method maintains 100% of the original model accuracy.
The defense remains effective across various models and datasets tested in the study.
The approach is compatible with and can be integrated into existing privacy protection mechanisms.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce a parameter-level shuffling and RNS-based defense to block Source Inference Attacks in Federated Learning.

title: CausalWrap: Model-Agnostic Causal Constraint Wrappers for Tabular Synthetic Data
summary: Tabular synthetic data generators are typically trained to match observational distributions, which can yield high conventional utility (e.g., column correlations, predictive accuracy) yet poor preservation of structural relations relevant to causal analysis and out-of-distribution (OOD) reasoning. When the downstream use of synthetic data involves causal reasoning -- estimating treatment effects, evaluating policies, or testing mediation pathways -- merely matching the observational distribution is insufficient: structural fidelity and treatment-mechanism preservation become essential. We propose CausalWrap (CW), a model-agnostic wrapper that injects partial causal knowledge (PCK) -- trusted edges, forbidden edges, and qualitative/monotonic constraints -- into any pretrained base generator (GAN, VAE, or diffusion model), without requiring access to its internals. CW learns a lightweight, differentiable post-hoc correction map applied to samples from the base generator, optimized with causal penalty terms under an augmented-Lagrangian schedule. We provide theoretical results connecting penalty-based optimization to constraint satisfaction and relating approximate factorization to joint distributional control. We validate CW on simulated structural causal models (SCMs) with known ground-truth interventions, semi-synthetic causal benchmarks (IHDP and an ACIC-style suite), and a real-world ICU cohort (MIMIC-IV) with expert-elicited partial graphs. CW improves causal fidelity across diverse base generators -- e.g., reducing average treatment effect (ATE) error by up to 63% on ACIC and lifting ATE agreement from 0.00 to 0.38 on the intensive care unit (ICU) cohort -- while largely retaining conventional utility.

processed.summary:
- **CausalWrap (CW)** is introduced as a model-agnostic wrapper designed to enforce causal constraints on tabular synthetic data generated by black-box models like GANs, VAEs, or diffusion models.
- The architecture employs a **lightweight, differentiable post-hoc correction map** that transforms base generator samples to align with **Partial Causal Knowledge (PCK)**, such as trusted edges and monotonic constraints.
- Optimization is achieved through **causal penalty terms** and an **augmented-Lagrangian schedule**, allowing for structural fidelity without requiring access to the internal weights of the base generator.
- The framework bridges the gap between observational distribution matching and **structural causal modeling**, ensuring synthetic data is reliable for downstream tasks like treatment effect estimation.
key_results: Reduced Average Treatment Effect (ATE) error by up to 63% on the ACIC-style causal benchmark suite.
Improved ATE agreement on the MIMIC-IV ICU cohort from a baseline of 0.00 to 0.38 using expert-elicited graphs.
Demonstrated successful preservation of structural relations across diverse base generators including VAEs and Diffusion models.
Maintained high conventional utility (column correlations and predictive accuracy) while significantly increasing causal fidelity.
Established theoretical guarantees connecting penalty-based optimization to joint distributional control and constraint satisfaction.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce CausalWrap, a model-agnostic wrapper that significantly improves causal reasoning and treatment effect accuracy in synthetic data.
